name: File a Habitability Complaint with the City Housing Department
description: Call the city or county housing / code-enforcement department to open an official habitability complaint after the landlord has failed to repair an urgent issue — opening an inspection record that preserves the tenant's legal posture for future rent-withholding, repair-and-deduct, or lease-break.
disclaimer: Not legal advice. Habitability + early-termination skills are scripted to PRESERVE legal posture (request things in writing, refuse verbal-only assurances) — but the operator must consult a tenant attorney for actual rights determination.

context.when_to_use: Landlord has been notified (in writing — see `report-urgent-maintenance`) of a habitability issue and has either: ignored it past a reasonable deadline (often 24–72 hours for emergencies; days to weeks for non-emergency code issues), refused to repair it, or repaired it badly enough that the issue persists. Use this BEFORE rent-withholding or lease-break — the inspection record is the foundation for either move.
preconditions: User has already reported the issue to the landlord IN WRITING (text or email) with a timestamp.
Landlord has either not responded, missed a stated deadline, or refused to repair.
User has documentation: photos, videos, the original report, any landlord response.
User has the name of the city / county housing department or code-enforcement agency. (If unknown, ask the operator to look it up first.)
User understands that an inspection may put the unit on a public record AND may prompt landlord retaliation (which is illegal in most places but does happen).

principles: An inspection record is leverage — once the city has cited the unit, the landlord's repair obligation is no longer optional.
Be factual, not emotional. Code-enforcement officers take notes that become evidence; opinions get filtered out, dates and conditions do not.

Anti-retaliation laws exist in most US jurisdictions — landlords cannot legally raise rent, refuse to renew, or evict in response to a code complaint. Mention it on the call only if asked; do not threaten.
Get the complaint number, the inspector's name, and the expected inspection window before hanging up. Without those three, you have nothing to follow up on.
If a city offers an anonymous complaint option, ask the operator first — anonymous filings sometimes have weaker enforcement and weaker retaliation protections than named ones.

phrases.opener: Hi, I'd like to file a habitability complaint against my landlord. The address is [full address, unit number]. The issue is [one-sentence description]. I reported it to the landlord in writing on [date] and the repair hasn't happened.
phrases.describe_issue: Specifically, [issue]. It started on [date]. The landlord was notified by [text/email] on [date], and as of today it's still [unrepaired / worsening / partially fixed and still failing].
phrases.ask_about_process: Can you walk me through what happens next — when an inspector comes out, whether I need to be home, and what the timeline is for the landlord to fix it after a citation?
phrases.ask_for_complaint_number: Could I get the complaint or case number for this so I can refer to it in any follow-up?
phrases.ask_about_retaliation_protection: If the landlord retaliates — like trying to raise rent or non-renew — is there a separate complaint I should file at that point?
phrases.ask_about_anonymous_option: Is filing anonymously an option, and does it change how the complaint is enforced?
phrases.graceful_close: Thank you. To confirm: case number [#], inspector or contact [name], inspection window [dates], next step on my side is [action]. I'll be available for the inspection.

tactics.name: Open with the address, the issue, and the prior-notice date
tactics.script: Use the `opener`. The prior-notice date is the single most important fact — it proves the landlord had a chance to fix it.

tactics.name: State facts in date order
tactics.when: When asked to describe what happened.
tactics.script: Use `describe_issue`. Walk it chronologically: when it started, when reported, what landlord said or didn't say, current condition. Stick to facts.

tactics.name: Pin the process
tactics.script: Use `ask_about_process`. Find out: inspection window, whether the tenant must be present, what the landlord's repair deadline becomes after citation, and what happens if the landlord doesn't comply.

tactics.name: Capture the case number
tactics.script: Use `ask_for_complaint_number`. Get the number, the inspector's name (if assigned), and the direct line for follow-up.

tactics.name: Pre-arm the retaliation complaint
tactics.when: If the user has expressed concern about landlord retaliation.
tactics.script: Use `ask_about_retaliation_protection`. Find out the path before retaliation happens — knowing it exists makes it easier to spot the moment it starts.

boundaries: Do NOT exaggerate or embellish the condition. Inspectors will see the unit themselves and a credibility loss hurts the entire case.
Do NOT promise to withhold rent in the same call. Rent-withholding has separate legal procedure — handle via `ask_operator` after a tenant attorney consult.
Do NOT name other tenants or share their issues without their permission.
Do NOT make claims about the landlord's other properties or other tenants — only your unit.
Do NOT discuss settlement amounts, lawsuits, or attorney involvement on the call. Code enforcement is a parallel track, not a negotiation venue.
success_signals: Complaint accepted, case number issued, inspector assigned.
Inspection window is within days, not weeks (true habitability issues are usually prioritised).
Officer explains the citation-and-cure timeline clearly.
failure_signals: Department says the issue isn't in their jurisdiction (call back to ask which agency handles it).
Officer is dismissive of a clear habitability issue — ask politely for a supervisor.
No case number issued — the complaint isn't real yet.
Inspection window quoted is weeks out for a true emergency (heat, water, sewage) — ask about expedited review.

exit_strategy.on_success: Confirm case number, inspector name (if known), inspection window, what the tenant must do (be home, document conditions, etc.), and the post-citation landlord timeline. Report all of this to the operator.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Ask which agency DOES handle it (housing inspection, public health, fire marshal, building department all overlap) and call that next. Report the runaround to the operator.
follow_ups: Operator emails landlord a copy of the complaint case number for the record (do this ONLY after talking to a tenant attorney if retaliation is a concern).
Calendar the inspection window — tenant must be reachable.
Photo / video the unit conditions the morning of the inspection so the timestamped record matches what the inspector sees.
After the inspection, request the inspector's written report — operator should follow up if not received within 5 business days.

required_user_info: Full unit address and unit number
City / county and the housing or code-enforcement department name + phone number
Date the issue started and date the landlord was notified in writing
Copy of the written notification (text screenshot, email)
Photos / video / readings documenting the condition
Whether the user is concerned about retaliation
